Title: Effects of repeated exposure to full portions of non-alcoholic beers at home over several weeks on sensory perception, satisfaction, wanting, and purchase intent
Authors: Takahiro Wakihira; Michel Visalli; Pascal Schlich
AbstractMini: Home-use testing of two non-alcoholic beers showed repeated exposure changed wanting and purchase intent differently by product, while non-users remained less satisfied than users after eight exposures.
WhyRelevant: Useful for NOLO product design: it links sensory profile to repeat adoption, clarifying what improves conversion of mainstream beer drinkers.

Title: Impact of different production methods on selected aroma compounds for non-alcoholic beers by developing an HS-SPME GC-MS method
Authors: Sönke Kienitz; Hubert Kollmannsberger; Annsophie Schreff; Dennis Mayer; Christoph Neugrodda; Thomas Becker
AbstractMini: Compares limited fermentation, special yeast, thermal dealcoholization and reverse osmosis for non-alcoholic beer using a calibrated HS-SPME GC-MS method for 50 aroma compounds plus sensory validation.
WhyRelevant: Directly actionable for NOLO process decisions because it ties production route to aroma retention, off-notes and measurable analytical methods.

Title: Toward a digital twin for beer quality control: development of a digital model integrating industrial process data and model-based fermentation descriptors
Authors: Colomba Dazzarola; Ronald Tighe; José R. Pérez-Correa; Pedro A. Saa
AbstractMini: A hybrid model calibrates beer fermentation kinetics from industrial data and combines them with classifiers and regression models to predict quality attributes before product completion.
WhyRelevant: Strong fit for brewery digitalization: it shows how process data and mechanistic descriptors can support earlier quality prediction and corrective action.

Title: Automated monitoring of alcoholic fermentation: trends and challenges
Authors: Tomáš Horváth; Szilárd Kun; László Sipos; Duc Trung Pham
AbstractMini: Review of automated fermentation monitoring with e-nose, e-tongue and other sensors. It argues current studies are fragmented, lack shared datasets and often apply ML below robust data-science standards.
WhyRelevant: Relevant for scaling smart fermentation because it identifies sensor gaps, data quality issues and the need for industrial-grade monitoring architectures.

Title: Brewery Byproducts as Bioenergy Sources: A Review of Biohydrogen and Biomethane Production
Authors: Larissa Oliveira da Silva; Fabrícia Maria Santana Silva; João Paulo Bassin
AbstractMini: Review of hydrogen and methane production from brewery spent grain, spent yeast, hot trub and brewery wastewater, with reported yield ranges and process constraints such as recalcitrance and inhibitors.
WhyRelevant: Useful for circularity strategy because it quantifies viable valorization pathways for brewery residues and links them to on-site energy recovery.

Title: Functional Valorization and Bioactivity Enhancement of Spent Coffee Grounds Through Lactic Acid Fermentation
AbstractMini: Lactic fermentation of spent coffee grounds with Lactobacillus plantarum increased viable counts and improved antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity alongside phenolic remodeling.
WhyRelevant: Shows a realistic upcycling route for beverage-side residues into higher-value functional ingredients using a fermentation process that is industrially familiar.

Title: Exploiting brewery wastewater for the sustainable cultivation of Chlorella fusca: Biomass productivity, biochemical composition, and production cost
Authors: Jenyfer de Almeida Conceição; Michele Greque de Morais; Etiele Greque de Morais; Jorge Alberto Vieira Costa
AbstractMini: Cultivating Chlorella fusca in brewery effluent enabled biomass production with balanced protein, carbohydrate and lipid composition while reducing estimated production cost from over 84 to about 18 EUR/kg.
WhyRelevant: Directly relevant to brewery effluent valorization because it couples wastewater treatment, biomass generation and cost reduction in one system.

Title: A critical review of biodegradable polymers for food packaging: Enhancing functionality and environmental sustainability
AbstractMini: Review of biodegradable food-packaging polymers covering material families, fabrication routes, barrier and antimicrobial functionality, intelligent sensing features and life-cycle considerations.
WhyRelevant: Relevant for packaging scouting because it maps which biodegradable systems are closing the performance gap with petro-plastics and where trade-offs remain.

Title: Smart Packaging 4.0: A Bibliometric Analysis of Sensor Integration, Food Safety, and Sustainability in Food Packaging Systems
Authors: Mochammad Jusuf Djafar; Huda M Elmatsani; S Joni Munarso; Jonni Firdaus; Sudarwaji Edi Yuwono Trihadi; Wahyu Purwanta; Budi Setiadi Sadikin; Sahlan Sahlan; Nasruddin Nasruddin; Lanjar Lanjar
AbstractMini: Bibliometric analysis of 253 documents shows smart packaging moving toward cyber-physical systems combining sensors, bioactive materials, IoT, AI and blockchain for traceability and food management.
WhyRelevant: Useful for packaging roadmap work because it surfaces converging technologies and commercialization barriers in intelligent packaging.

Title: New frontiers in beverage fortification: Delivery of functional ingredients via micro- and nanoencapsulation in dairy and non-dairy beverages
Authors: Mohammad Rezaul Islam Shishir; Suliman Khan; Naymul Karim; Muhammad Bilal; Sarfaraz Hussain; Md Saifullah; Ahmed K Rashwan; Haroon Elrasheid Tahir; Isam A Mohamed Ahmed; Xinhua Zhang; Fujun Li
AbstractMini: Review of micro- and nanoencapsulation systems for fortified beverages, showing better stability, bioaccessibility, probiotic survivability and flavor masking, with unresolved scale-up and regulatory issues.
WhyRelevant: Relevant for functional beverage innovation because it clarifies practical delivery systems for bioactives and the barriers to commercial translation.

Title: Exploring Origanum dictamnus as a functional food ingredient: Phytochemical characterization and gut motility modulation
Authors: Charikleia Paloukopoulou; Theofano Psarra; Maria Koutsoviti-Papadopoulou; Anastasia Karioti
AbstractMini: An herbal beverage infusion was profiled by NMR and HPLC-PDA-MS and showed spasmolytic activity in ex vivo gut tissues, supporting digestive functional-food positioning.
WhyRelevant: Useful as a signal for gut-comfort beverages because it combines phytochemical evidence with a physiological activity model rather than a purely marketing claim.
